coredata.dc:description: In order to enhance the visible light catalytic activity of TiO2 by broadening its photoresponse range, the TiO2/N-GQDs (TGs) composite catalysts with a three-dimensional hollow urchin-like structure were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The microstructure and photoelectric properties of the synthesized TGs composites were analyzed through a series of characterizations, and the performance in degrading organic dyes under visible light was investigated. The results demonstrated that the prepared composites effectively enhanced the visible light absorption spectrum of TiO2 by forming heterojunction. The degradation efficiency of organic dyes could reach 95.71% under visible light irradiation for 1 h, which further increased to 99.39% after 2 h. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation process could be effectively accelerated under acid conditions. After 5 cycles of testing, the degradation efficiency of TGs composites on organic dyes was hardly reduced, indicating that they had great visible light catalytic activity and significant reusability.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of hollow urchin-like TiO2/N-GQDs composites and efficient photocatalytic degradation research on waste organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang 330099, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Sciences, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang 330099, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang 330099, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to enhance the visible light catalytic activity of TiO2 by broadening its photoresponse range, the TiO2/N-GQDs (TGs) composite catalysts with a three-dimensional hollow urchin-like structure were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The microstructure and photoelectric properties of the synthesized TGs composites were analyzed through a series of characterizations, and the performance in degrading organic dyes under visible light was investigated. The results demonstrated that the prepared composites effectively enhanced the visible light absorption spectrum of TiO2 by forming heterojunction. The degradation efficiency of organic dyes could reach 95.71% under visible light irradiation for 1 h, which further increased to 99.39% after 2 h. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation process could be effectively accelerated under acid conditions. After 5 cycles of testing, the degradation efficiency of TGs composites on organic dyes was hardly reduced, indicating that they had great visible light catalytic activity and significant reusability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of the printing and dyeing industry, a large number of organic dyes and other pollutants are discharged into freshwater resources, seriously damaging the ecological environment [1]. Water pollution leads directly to the serious shortage of fresh water resources. According to the World Water Council, nearly 3.8 billion people will face water scarcity by 2030 [2].The organic dyes in printing and dyeing wastewater are difficult to achieve direct degradation because of their stable properties, high chroma and complex structure, which is an urgent water pollution problem [3]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop efficient, stable and environmentally friendly methods to remove pollutants from wastewater. At present, the common methods for organic dye removal in industry are membrane separation, adsorption, chemical and electrochemical precipitation, acoustic oxidation and photocatalysis [4]. Among them, photocatalysis is an efficient and environmentally friendly pollution treatment technology, which can completely mineralize organic matter and degrade them into carbon dioxide and water through light-induced reactions, thereby avoiding secondary pollution. And numerous researches of semiconductor photocatalysis technology have been studied on the treatment of organic dye wastewater [5,6].

Nowadays, different types of semiconductor photocatalysts have been studied extensively, such as TiO2 [7], WO3 [8], MgO [9] and Bi2O3 [10]. Among them, TiO2 photocatalyst is widely used in sewage treatment and other fields because of its good stability and strong catalytic ability [11]. However, TiO2 has the problems of narrow response wavelength range and low light energy utilization rate in practical application, which greatly limits its application range [12]. Recent studies have shown that the absorption spectrum of TiO2 can be expanded to visible light range through metal and non-metal doping, organic dye sensitization and coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors [13,14]. For example, Deshmukh et al. [15] prepared TiO2-ZnO nanocomposites, which effectively improved the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of methyl orange than neat TiO2 under ultraviolet light. However, the utilization of visible light for composites is still relatively low. Ahamed et al. [16] prepared TiO2/CdS/Pd composites, which effectively enhanced the degradation efficiency of TiO2 on organic dyes and toxic metal ions under visible light, but the composites could only play a good role when H2O2 was used as an auxiliary catalyst. Tang et al. [17] prepared N-TiO2/graphene composites, which broadened the absorption range of TiO2 to visible light and effectively improved the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of organic dyes. However, the composites were synthesized by vapour-thermal method, and the preparation process was more complicated. Li et al. [18] prepared TiO2/graphene composites by a simple hydrothermal method, which exhibited the capability of photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes under visible light irradiation. However, there was still room for improvement in terms of the degradation efficiency.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a zero-dimensional nanocrystalline material, exhibit excellent photostability, biocompatibility, water solubility and low toxicity [19]. Moreover, The GQDs possess a diminutive particle size and facile compatibility with other materials, rendering them suitable as co-catalysts in the realm of photocatalysis. [20,21]. There are many carbon sources for the synthesis of GQDs [22], such as coffee grounds, citric acid, glucose, starch and bagasse, etc. For example, Masteri-Farahani et al. [23] prepared GQDs using citric acid as carbon source by hydrothermal method, which could be applied in the field of fluorescent nanosensor. Shi et al. [24] prepared GQDs using glucose as carbon source and applied it to the field of anticancer drug. Kashani et al. [25] prepared GQDs using citric acid as carbon source by pyrolysis method, which could be applied to in-vitro cell imaging. Despite these advances, the potential of TiO2/N-GQDs composites as photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants has not been fully explored.

In this work, the three-dimensional hollow urchin-like TiO2/N-GQDs (TGs) composites were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using potassium titanium oxalate and citric acid as raw materials. In this way, N-GQDs could embed into the inside of TiO2 microspheres, and the contact area between them was greatly increased, thus forming more stable heterojunctions and providing more active sites. The structural and photoelectric properties of the TGs composites were characterized using XRD, FTIR, XPS, TG, SEM/TEM and UV–vis, etc. techniques. The degradation efficiency of organic dyes, including Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), methyl red (MR) and Congo red (CR), was investigated by varying the N-GQDs doping amount, reaction time, catalyst dosage and solution pH value. Additionally, the reusability of the prepared composites was evaluated through 5 cyclics testing. The construction of TGs composite photocatalyst would play a crucial role in guiding the development of efficient, simple-prepared and reusable visible light catalysts for treating organic dye wastewater.

Potassium titanium oxalate (K2TiO(C2O4)2·2H2O) and urea (H2NCONH2) were purchased from Tianjin Zhonglian Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Diethylene glycol (C4H10O3) and citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), methyl red (MR) and congo red (CR) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. All chemical materials were analytical grade and were used directly without any pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of three-dimensional hollow urchin-like TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.8 g potassium titanium oxalate and 2.4 g urea were added into 20 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. Subsequently, 60 mL diethylene glycol was introduced into the mixed solution and continued to stir for 30 min under room temperature. Then the mixed solution was swiftly transferred into an autoclave liner and reacted hydrothermally at 180 ℃ for 12 h [26]. The precipitation was washed repeatedly with water and anhydrous ethanol to remove the organic residue and dried at 70 ℃.

First, 2.1 g urea and 2.4 g citric acid were added into 48 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. Then the mixed solution was quickly poured into autoclave liner, and the N-GQDs solution could be obtained by hydrothermally reacting at 160 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the N-GQDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain N-GQDs solid. The synthesis mechanism of N-GQDs was shown in Fig. 1 .

Firstly, 0.8 g potassium titanium oxalate and 2.4 g urea were added into 20 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. Next, 60 mL diethylene glycol and different mass of citric acid were added into the mixed solution and continued to stir for 30 min under room temperature. Then the mixed solution was quickly poured into autoclave liner and reacted hydrothermally at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the brown precipitate was obtained after centrifugation and was washed by anhydrous ethanol and deionized water orderly. Finally, the three-dimensional hollow urchin-like TiO2/N-GQDs microspheres were obtained after precipitation drying. The composites were named as TGs1-TGs6 based on the mass variation of citric acid, and the preparation process of TGs composites was shown in Fig. 2 .

The crystal phase of TGs catalyst was analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, TD-3500, Dandong Mada Technology Co., LTD.), and the diffraction angle 2θ range was 10-90°. The functional groups and elemental compositions of TGs composites were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Scientific K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.). The microstructure of TGs composites were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 300, ZEISS, Germany). The photoelectric properties of TGs composites was tested by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS, UV-3600i Plus, Shimadazu Corporation, Japan), photoluminescence spectra (PL, FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd.). The thermal stability of TGs composites was tested by thermogravimetric analysis (TG, Discovery TGA 550, USA). The potential and average size of TGs composites were measured by Malvern workstation (NANOZS90, Malvern Panalytical Ltd.). The 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) was used as a visible light source, equipped with 420 nm cut-off filter. The absorbance of organic dyes was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-T6, Shanghai Yi-Spectrum Instruments Co., Ltd.).

In the photocatalysis experiment, 15 mg photocatalyst powder was added into 50 mL solution containing organic dye at a concentration of 10 mg/L. The mixture was gently stirred in darkness for 60 min to establish an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the reaction was conducted under xenon lamp illumination. A liquid sample of 2 mL was extracted every 20 min and filtered through a 22 μm filter membrane to measure the absorbance. The standard curve of organic dyes was established by Lambert-Beer law (Equation (1) to calculate the concentration of organic dye solution during degradation. The Equation (2) was employed to calculate the degradation efficiency of organic dyes.

where A is the absorbance of the organic dye solution, T is the transmittance, k is the molar absorptivity, b is the thickness of absorption layer. η is the degradation efficiency, C 0 is the initial concentration and C t is the solution concentrations after degradation time t.

Fig. 3 displayed the XRD patterns of the N-GQDs, TiO2 and TGs composites. The diffraction peaks observed at 2θ values of 24.54°, 36.97°, 47.25°, 53.62°, 54.58° and 63.05° were attributed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) crystal planes of anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1272), respectively. Additionally, the diffraction peak at the 2θ value of 30.8° corresponded to the (121) crystal planes of brookite phase TiO2 (JCPDS 29–1360) [27]. This indicated that the prepared TiO2 was in the mixed crystal phase, which had more excellent photocatalytic performance [28]. The characteristic peaks of the prepared TGs composites were found to be similar to those observed in neat TiO2, while no discernible characteristic peaks associated with N-GQDs were detected. This observation indicated that the introduction of N-GQDs did not change the crystal structure of TiO2. The position of the characteristic peaks in the TGs composites did not shift, indicating that N-GQDs dispersed on the surface of TiO2 and did not enter the TiO2 lattice [29]. However, excessive content of N-GQDs led to a reduction in the crystallinity of the composites, as evidenced by the patterns of TGs6. The diffraction peak belonging to graphene (002) in N-GQDs was not clearly seen in the composites, which might be due to the high dispersion of N-GQDs in the TGs composites, or it was shielded by the anatase TiO2 peak (101) [30,31]. The average crystallite sizes of TiO2,TGs2, TGs4, TGs6 and N-GQDs were calculated by the Debye-Scherrer equation to be approximately 113.5 Å, 117.5 Å, 137.3 Å, 97.3 Å and 6.0 Å, respectively [32], which indicated that TGs4 had the highest electrical conductivity [33].

Fig. 4 represented the FTIR spectra of the N-GQDs, TiO2 and TGs composites. It was evident that the neat TiO2 exhibited distinct peaks within the wide absorption range around 500–750 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O bonds in anatase TiO2. And this peak was also observed in the spectra of all TGs composites. In addition, all spectra exhibited distinct peaks at approximately 1627 cm−1 and 3428 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups and surface physisorption of water [34]. For neat N-GQDs, the absorption peak at 1550 cm−1 was belonged to the characteristic absorption of CC bond and the tensile vibration of CO bond in COOH. The absorption peak at 3150 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic absorption of NH and CH bonds, respectively [32,35]. The characteristic peaks of TiO2 and N-GQDs existed in the FTIR spectra of TGs2 and TGs4 composites, which proved the successful preparation of the composites. The results showed that the N atom was successfully doped on the GQDs, and the prepared TiO2/N-GQDs had the functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen, which made a good hydrophilicity for composites and effectively enhanced its photocatalysis.

The surface microstructure of the synthesized TiO2 and TGs composites were shown in Fig. 5 (a-d). The diameter of the prepared TiO2 microspheres was about 1 μm, and there are many nano-burrs distributed outside it. Some of the TiO2 microspheres showed obvious hollow urchin-like structure, which could effectively increase its specific surface area. The morphology of TGs composites was very similar to TiO2, and no significant differences could be seen. Because the particles of N-GQDs were very small, their morphology cannot be directly observed. Therefore, the existence and distribution of C and N elements belonging to N-GQDs in TGs composites were determined by EDS spectrum and element distribution tests. It could be seen from Fig. 5(e-h) that TGs4 composites contained Ti, O, C and N elements, and their distributions were uniform, which proved that N-GQDs was successfully supported on the surface of TiO2.

Fig. 6 further displayed the microstructure of TGs4 composites by TEM. As shown in Fig. 6(a-b), the TGs4 microspheres showed an urchin-like structure, which was consistent with SEM characterization results. The inside of the microsphere was made up of densely packed nanoparticles, while the outside was made up of overlapping nanoribbons. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the surface of TiO2 exhibited numerous black spots, approximately 10 nm in diameter, which were tentatively identified as N-GQDs. By the study of high-magnification diagram, as shown in Fig. 6(d), it could be seen that there were two different lattice stripes on the surface of TGs4 composites. N-GQDs corresponded to lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which proved the presence of graphite and was consistent with the graphene (100) diffraction plane [33]. In addition, the 0.35 nm lattice fringe was attributed to the presence of TiO2, corresponding to the (101) lattice plane of anatase phase TiO2 [17]. This was consistent with XRD results. Based on the above SEM and TEM characterization results, it could be concluded that N-GQDs were closely distributed on the surface of hollow urchin-like TiO2 microspheres, indicating that TGs4 composite microspheres had been successfully prepared. Additionally, the formation of heterojunctions between TiO2 and N-GQDs enabled the composite microspheres to effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and prolong their lifetime.

The chemical composition and surface properties of TGs composites were determined by XPS analysis, and the results were shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7(a), The TGs4 composites exhibited distinct peaks in the full spectrum, corresponding to TiO2 (Ti and O) and N-GQDs (C and N). The peaks observed at 284.6 eV in the TiO2 spectrum could be attributed to the incorporation of a carbon source during calibration testing. And the peaks observed at 464.18 eV and 458.5 eV in Fig. 7(b) corresponded to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 of TiO2, respectively. However, these two peaks in TGs4 composites exhibited a shift towards low binding energy, which could be attributed to the strong interaction between N-GQDs and the interfaces of TiO2. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s was depicted in Fig. 7(c). In the spectrum of TGs4 composites, the peaks observed at 529.22, 529.93, and 531.09 eV could be attributed to Ti-O, CO, and OH bonds, respectively [33]. Fig. 7(d) showed the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s. The TGs4 composites exhibited three distinct carbon peaks specifically at 284.26, 285.79, and 287.68 eV, corresponding to sp2 CC and CC bonds, sp3 CO bonds and CO bonds respectively. These peaks were primarily attributed to the contribution of N-GQDs [36]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s was depicted in Fig. 7(e), three peaks at 398.39, 399.62, and 400.65 eV were observed for TGs4 composite. The peaks at 398.39 eV and 399.62 eV corresponded to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N structures respectively, primarily attributed to the substitution of nitro nitrogen by lattice oxygen [37]. And the peak at 400.65 eV was corresponded to the NH band, indicating that GQDs were doped with nitrogen derived from urea during synthesis [32].

The thermal degradation behavior of neat TiO2 and TGs composites was tested by thermogravimetric analyzer. The TGA and DTG curves of neat TiO2 and TGs composites under N2 atmosphere were shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b) respectively. After heat treatment at 800 ℃, the residual rate of TiO2, TGs2, TGs4 and TGs6 was 94.69 wt%, 87.48 wt%, 75.40 wt% and 57.12 wt%, respectively. The decomposition behavior of TGs composites could be observed to occur in three distinct stages. In the initial stage, weightlessness was observed at 30–180 ℃ due to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules. As the temperature further increased to 220–340 ℃, graphene oxide underwent rapid oxidation into CO2 or its thermally unstable organic groups was decomposed [38]. As the temperature further increased to 405–500 ℃, the significant weight loss was attributed to the exothermic oxidation of oxygen-containing functional groups and the decomposition of the graphite skeleton [39]. However, the decomposition temperature of these three stages of the TGs6 composites was significantly higher than that of TGs2 and TGs4, potentially attributed to the influence of van der Waals forces, resulting in a dense arrangement of N-GQDs. With the increase of N-GQDs supported on the TiO2 surface, this dense filler was significantly strengthened, resulting in a gradual elevation of the decomposition temperature [40].

In addition, the weight losses of the prepared TGs composites could be measured to estimate the actual mass content of N-GQDs. By disregarding any negligible amount of adsorbed water on the surface of TGs composites, the actual mass percentage of N-GQDs in TGs composites could be calculated and presented in Table 1 . The results showed that the N-GQDs content of TGs2, TGs4 and TGs6 composites was 7.21 wt%, 19.29 wt% and 37.57 wt%, respectively.

Fig. 9 showed the PL spectra of neat TiO2, N-GQDs and TGs4 composites under 320 nm excitation wavelength. Generally, the lower PL intensity generally signified superior photocarrier separation capability and better photocatalytic activity [41]. N-GQDs showed a wide range of fluorescence emission peaks from 400 to 700 nm, indicating of the large number of generation and recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The PL emission intensity of N-GQDs was significantly higher than that of neat TiO2, while that of TGs4 modified with N-GQDs was obviously lower than that of neat TiO2 and N-GQDs. This result suggested that the efficiency of charge separation and transfer in TiO2/N-GQDs composites surpassed that of neat TiO2 and N-GQDs. Moreover, the formation of heterojunction between TiO2 and N-GQDs significantly reduced the recombination rate of photogenerated holes (h+) and electrons (e−) in the TGs composites, thereby enhancing electron injection efficiency. The above results indicated that the photoelectron transfer efficiency could be effectively improved by modifying N-GQDs on TiO2.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the neat TiO2, N-GQDs and TGs composites were shown in Fig. 10 (a). N-GQDs exhibited stronger absorption compared to neat TiO2 in the spectral range of 200–700 nm. Additionally, the absorption sideband of TGs composites was redshifted relative to the band edge of TiO2 due to the recombination effect and oxygen vacancy defect, which might be due to the introduction of N-GQDs [42]. The absorption strength of TGs4 composites in the visible region of 600–800 nm was enhanced compared to N-GQDs, which might be caused by the synergistic effect of TiO2 and N-GQDs. In addition, the absorption of all TGs composites in the visible light region was significantly enhanced compared to neat TiO2, and the TGs4 composites had stronger absorption in both the ultraviolet and visible regions. These results further indicated that the N-GQDs were successfully supported on TiO2, and the TGs composites had higher light energy utilization than neat TiO2 and N-GQDs.

Furthermore, the Kubelka-Munk Transformation (Equation (3) could be used to estimate the bandgap energy of the TiO2, N-GQDs and TGs4 composites. Subsequently, the Equation (4) and (5) were used to calculate the positions of TiO2 and N-GQDs in terms of valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB).

where α is the absorption coefficient, h represents Planck’s constant, ν refers to frequency of the light, A is the proportional constant, E g is the energy of band gap, E VB stands for the edge potential of VB, χ is the electronegativity of the semiconductor (about 5.81 eV for TiO2 [43]), E e represents the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV vs. NHE) and E CB is the edge potential of CB.

As shown in Fig. 10(b), the calculated E g values of neat TiO2, N-GQDs and TGs4 composites were 3.20, 2.44 and 3.01 eV, respectively, and the E VB value of TiO2 was calculated to be 2.91 eV. According to previous study, the E VB of N-GQDs was about 1.41 eV [44]. Consequently, the E CB values of TiO2 and N-GQDs were estimated to be −1.03 and −0.29 eV, respectively. Through the above analysis, the values of E CB, E VB and E g for TiO2 and N-GQDs at the point of zero charge were summarized in Table 2 .

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a common method for analyzing photogenerated carrier separation capability. The impedance spectra of neat TiO2 and TGs composites were shown in Fig. 11 . Compared with neat TiO2, the arc radius of TGs composite was significantly reduced, indicating that the charge carrier of TGs composite had lower transfer resistance and faster charge transfer kinetics [45]. It could also be attributed to the construction of efficient carrier transfer structure at the TiO2 and N-GQDs heterojunction interfaces. Among the TGs composites, the TGs4 had the lowest impedance value, indicating that it had the highest photocarrier separation ability and the fastest charge transfer ability, which could effectively improve its photocarrier life and photocatalyst activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential and particle size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The zeta potential serves as a crucial parameter for assessing the stability of colloidal dispersion systems. When the value of zeta potential approaches 0 mV, there is minimal repulsion between nanoparticles, leading to their tendency to aggregate. Conversely, when the absolute value of the zeta potential exceeds 30 mV, nanoparticles exhibit a propensity for dispersion. The zeta potential values of the neat TiO2, N-GQDs and TGs composites were shown in Fig. 12 (a). It could be seen that the absolute zeta potential values of TiO2 and N-GQDs were 19.10 and 8.88 mV respectively, which were much smaller than 30 mV, indicating that their disperse system was unstable and easy to agglomerate, which was not conducive to photocatalytic reaction. The absolute zeta potential values of TGs composites increased with the increase of N-GQDs content, and the values of TGs2, TGs4 and TGs6 were 30.83, 37.13 and 46.70 mV respectively, which might be due to the coordination effect of N-GQDs and TiO2. The results showed that the TGs composites had a good and stable dispersion system.

The analysis of particle size is used to assess the average size of particles in samples. In general, the smaller the particle size of the photocatalyst, the larger its specific surface area, resulting in a reduced space charge effect and shorter diffusion time for photocarriers to reach the surface. And this property is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 12(b), the particle size of TiO2 and N-GQDs was 1145.33 nm and 6.54 nm respectively, which was consistent with SEM and TEM results. Compared with neat TiO2, the particle size of TGs composite gradually decreased with the increase of N-GQDs content, and the particle size of TGs2, TGs4 and TGs6 was 1073, 974.5 and 768.97 nm, respectively. The results showed that the introduction of N-GQDs could effectively reduce the particle size of TiO2, so that TGs composite had a larger specific surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation efficiency on different organic dyes
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the distinct chromaticity of various organic dyes, this study employed RhB and MB dyes at a concentration of 10 mg/L, MR and CR dyes at a concentration of 50 mg/L as target degradation materials to investigate the photocatalytic degradation performance of TGs catalysts. As shown in Fig. 13 , the TGs composites exhibited excellent photocatalytic efficiency for various organic dyes under visible light irradiation. And the degradation efficiencies of RhB, MB, CR, and MR solutions were 99.39 %, 99.16 %, 97.62 %, and 94.65 % respectively after 120 min of irradiation. The subsequent experiments selected 10 mg/L RhB organic dye as degradation target due to the highest degradation efficiency among different organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic efficiency of different catalysts on RhB
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation curves of RhB solution with different catalysts were presented in Fig. 14 (a). It could be observed that when the dark reaction time was 40 min, all samples had reached adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The maximum adsorption capacities of TiO2, TGs1, TGs2, TGs3, TGs4, TGs5 and TGs6 for RhB were 4.75 %, 6.77 %, 4.75 %, 4.23 %, 7.73 %, 11.58 % and 2.22 %, respectively. This slight difference might be due to the heterogeneous morphology of the catalyst. In addition, N-GQDs had little absorption of RhB due to its small particle size. When the duration of visible light exposure reached 120 min, the degradation efficiency of neat TiO2 on RhB was found to be only 24.45 % due to its limited absorption capacity in the visible light range. Similarly, the degradation efficiency of N-GQD on RhB was just 20.95 %, which could be attributed to its diminutive particle size, thereby impeding its capacity for adsorption and degradation of RhB. The degradation efficiency of TGs composites on RhB was greatly enhanced compared to neat TiO2. And the enhancement could be attributed to the incorporation of N-GQDs, which effectively extended the optical absorption range of TiO2 into the visible light region. Among all TGs composites, TGs4 composites showed the highest degradation efficiency on RhB under visible light, and the degradation efficiency of RhB reached 95.71 % after 60 min irradiation, and reached 99.39 % after 120 min irradiation. The degradation efficiency of different catalysts was ranked as follows: TGs4 > TGs3 > TGs2 > TGs1 > TGs5 > TGs6.

In order to further study the degradation of RhB by composite materials, a first-order reaction kinetics model was used to conduct a kinetic analysis of the process. The Equation was as follows:
(6)
ln ( C /
C 0
) = - k t

Where C 0 is the initial concentration and C is the solution concentration of RhB after degradation time t, and k is the apparent kinetic rate constant. As shown in Fig. 14(b), through calculation, the k values of TiO2, N-GQDs, TGs1, TGs2, TGs3, TGs4, TGs5 and TGs6 were 0.00221, 0.00195, 0.03507, 0.03667, 0.03673, 0.04119, 0.02982 and 0.02055 min−1, respectively. The TGs4 composites exhibited a significantly highest apparent kinetic constant among all TGs composites, which was 18.64 times that of neat TiO2.

The above results showed that when the content of N-GQDs in the TGs composites gradually increased, the specific surface area and the effective area of heterojunction would also increase, thus providing more active sites, promoting the separation of photogenic carriers, and improving the photocatalytic activity. However, when the content of N-GQDs in the composites was higher than 19.29 wt% of TGs4, the degradation efficiency of RhB decreased. This might be due to the fact that the high content of N-GQDs in the TGs composites would accelerate the recombination of electrons and holes in the CB, resulting in a decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, the TGs4 composites could be regarded as the optimal photocatalyst in this study and was suitable for subsequent experimental applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic efficiency of catalysts with dosage on RhB
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of various catalyst mass concentrations on the degradation performance of RhB were shown in Fig. 15 . When the mass concentration of catalyst was 0.1 g/L, 0.2 g/L, 0.3 g/L, 0.4 g/L and 0.5 g/L, the maximum adsorption capacity of RhB was 5.72 %, 10.71 %, 7.20 %, 14.12 % and 16.05 %, respectively. Under the same lighting conditions, the degradation efficiency of RhB solution was gradually improved with the increase of catalyst dosage. Because the high mass concentration promoted the adsorption of dye molecules and provided more active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, thus enhancing the adsorption and degradation efficiency of RhB. However, when the mass concentration of the catalyst exceeded 0.3 g/L, the degradation efficiency of RhB did not improve significantly. This might be due to the blocking, reflection and scattering of catalyst particles on light, and the increase of light energy loss, which made the degradation efficiency of RhB reached a relatively stable state. Therefore, the TGs4 photocatalyst should ideally choose a mass concentration of 0.3 g/L to achieve both cost savings and excellent catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic efficiency of RhB under different initial pH
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 16 (a) showed the effect of the initial pH of RhB solution on the photocatalytic degradation process. When the initial pH of RhB solution was 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10, the maximum adsorption capacity of RhB was 10.62 %, 9.13 %, 14.38 %, 8.96 % and 12.11 %, respectively. The results showed that pH value had little effect on adsorption in dark reaction. Under visible light, it could be seen that TGs4 photocatalyst had a high degradation efficiency when RhB solution was in acidic condition (up to 100 %), and the degradation efficiency gradually decreased with the increase of pH value. As shown in Fig. 16(b), when the pH value of RhB solution was 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10, the corresponding k value was 0.14917, 0.12158, 0.06966, 0.00892, and 0.00618 min−1, respectively. Among them, the TGs4 composites had a highest degradation kinetic constant when the pH value of RhB was 2, which was 3.62 times that of the initial pH value (Fig. 14(a)). The above results indicated that the photocatalyst was more effective in adsorbing and degrading dye molecules under acidic conditions, but less effective under alkaline conditions. This was because that there were a large number of hydrogen ions (H+) in the acidic system, and superoxide radicals (O2 −) were easy to react with H+ to generate lots of major active oxidizing substances such as peroxy radical (OOH) and hydroxyl radicals (OH), which effectively promoted the mineralization process of RhB molecules. Conversely, high pH would reduce the production of H2O2 during the reaction process, which reduced the production of free radicals (e.g., O2 − and OH), ultimately leading to a decrease in the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation [46].

The degradation efficiency of TGs4 composites was compared with other photocatalysts for organic dyes in Table 3 . The light sources of all the photocatalysts in the table were tested using visible light with a wavelength range of 420–780 nm. The photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst was comprehensively evaluated based on the type and concentration of dye solution, mass concentration of catalyst, irradiation time, and degradation efficiency. The results indicate that the TGs4 composites synthesized in this study exhibit a significantly higher degradation efficiency for the same dye compared to other catalysts, thus demonstrating their potential as effective photocatalysts.

Fig. 17 showed the repeated degradation efficiency of RhB using TGs4 photocatalyst. It could be seen that during 5 cycles of experiments, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TGs4 composite on RhB was hardly decreased, which was 99.82 %, 99.74 %, 99.12 %, 98.77 % and 97.64 %, respectively. The results showed that the TGs4 photocatalyst had excellent stability and reusability in the degradation of RhB solution. This could be attributed to the following reasons: Firstly, the hollow urchin-like structure enhanced the specific surface area of the photocatalyst and the adsorption capacity of dye molecules. Secondly, by in-situ growth method, the generated N-GQDs could be effectively embedded into the inside of TiO2 to form lots of stable heterojunctions that were not easy to fall off, which played an important role in the photodegradation process.

The photocatalytic mechanism of TiO2/N-GQDs composites was analyzed according to the above experiment. As shown in Fig. 18 , when the energy of irradiated light was equal to or greater than the energy of N-GQDs band gap (E g), photogenerated electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs were generated on N-GQDs. The electrons in its VB were excited and jumped to the CB, and then transferred to the CB of TiO2, thus accelerating charge separation. Subsequently, the efficient separation of charge promoted the production of superoxide radicals (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (OH), which could further degrade RhB into low molecular inorganic compounds. And the remaining h+ in the N-GQDs VB would also degrade RhB. The photocatalytic oxidation mechanism of TGs was as follows [52]:
(7) TGs + hν → e− + h+
(8) O2 + e- → O2 −
(9)
O2 − + H+ → OOH
(10) 2OOH → H2O2 + O2
(11) H2O2 + e− → OH + OH−
(12) H2O + h+ → OH + H+
(13) OH– + h+ → OH
(14) h+, OH, O2– + RhB → CO2 +H2O

Moreover, the hollow urchin-like structure enhanced the specific surface area and adsorption capacity of photocatalysts, while also increased light absorption through multiple reflections within the cavity.

In summary, the TGs composites with hollow urchin-like structure had been successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The special structure effectively increased the specific surface area of TGs composites and provided more active sites. The presence of heterojunctions between TiO2 and N-GQDs significantly reduced the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and expanded the photoresponse range of TiO2 to visible light. A series of characterizations and tests had proven that TGs composites possessed excellent photoelectric properties. The experimental results demonstrated that the TGs4 composites exhibited great efficiency in degrading organic dyes. The degradation efficiency of RhB reached 95.71 % under visible light irradiation for 1 h, and further increased to 99.39 % after 2 h. The degradation efficiency of MB, MR and CR were 99.16 %, 97.62 % and 94.65, respectively. The TGs4 composites exhibited a significantly highest apparent kinetic constant among all TGs composites, which was 18.64 times that of neat TiO2. Under acidic conditions, the generation of free radicals could be accelerated to further improve the degradation efficiency. And the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TGs4 on RhB could reach up to 100 % when the pH value of the RhB solution was 2 and the mass concentration of the photocatalyst was 0.3 g/L. In addition, the TGs4 composites had favorable stability and reusability, and the degradation efficiency of RhB could still reach to 97.64 % after the fifth cycle experiment. According to the photocatalytic mechanism, the high degradation efficiency could be attributed to the oxidation of dye molecules by O2 − and OH generated from photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the TGs composites, as well as the h+ in the VB of N-GQDs. Therefore, this study provided a green, simple and economical method for the treatment of organic dye-containing wastewater. The development of TGs composite also played a crucial role in guiding the creation of efficient and stable catalysts under visible light.
